text: The surgeon uses a vessel-sealing and cutting instrument to mobilize the rectosigmoid colon from its left lateral attachment, carefully cutting through the fat while avoiding opening the retroperitoneum. The inferior pelvic ligament and ureter become visible during this process.

In the pelvic region, the surgeon encounters flimsy adhesions likely due to pelvic inflammatory disease. Small energy gaps are used to manage these adhesions. The surgeon applies traction and pressure on the tissues with the grasper and blades, adjusting the energy settings to cut or seal vessels based on tissue vascularity.

The surgeon begins the hysterectomy by removing the fallopian tube, then identifies the utero-ovarian pedicle, which includes the round ligament, utero-ovarian ligament, and utero-ovarian vessels. The round ligament is cut with minimal energy application, followed by sealing the uterine vessels using a multifunctional instrument.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer instrument to cut a small vessel extending to the round ligament, while addressing appendiceal adhesions to the tube. The instrument seals the vessel by applying pressure and energy before cutting, ensuring preservation of the salpingial vessels and ovarian blood supply.

The surgeon opens the broad ligaments using a V manipulator inside the uterus, performing lateral, anterior, and posterior dissection. The posterior peritoneum is reflected to the level of the intracellular attachment. Due to pelvic inflammatory disease and previous cesarean section, there is minimal separation between the bladder and uterus. The surgeon opens the broad ligament laterally, identifies the uterine vessels, and bares the cervix below the bladder attachment at the scar level, then de-peritonealizes the upper part.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel for bladder dissection in a patient with a previous cesarean section, carefully opening the vault. The active lead is positioned upwards, and traction is applied using a grasper, facilitating the dissection despite limited vascularity and absence of clear tissue planes.

The surgeon opens the scar area from the lateral side, creating a space that joins the center. Traction is applied by the manipulator and grasper on the uterus, revealing the uterine vessels and vein. The scar area is opened to release the bladder from dense adhesions on the lower uterine segment, with fibrotic tissue from the scar being allowed to remain on the bladder. The same procedure is performed on the right side, identifying the lateral window and lifting the bladder slightly on the scar.

The surgeon releases the bladder laterally and centrally from surgical adhesions, with some cervical scar tissue remaining on the bladder. The pink bladder tissue is visible, along with its vascularity. The cervix is seen laterally on both sides, and the uterine vessels are clearly visible. The surgeon continues to work on both hard and soft tissue areas to reflect scar tissue onto the bladder.

The surgeon carefully dissects in the vesicovaginal space, releasing the bladder from adhesions and pushing it downward. The bladder is lifted, revealing the sacro-vaginal space, with visible cartilaginous tissue on the bladder. The cervix and upper vagina are exposed, highlighting the challenging dissection due to previous cesarean section adhesions.

The surgeon reflects the posterior leaf of the pericardium, exposing the uterine vessels during lateral dissection. Using an ultrasonic scalpel, the surgeon seals and cuts at the internal os level, lateralizing the stump until reaching the attachment of the utero-sacral ligament. Equal desiccation of tissues is maintained on both sides to prevent backflow bleeding.

The surgeon begins cutting at the internal os level on the right side, lateralizing the uterine stump to the utero-sacral level. The utero-sacral ligaments are clearly visible. The uterine manipulator is removed, and a myoma spiral is placed at the left upper quadrant of the uterus. The uterus is pulled towards the right port, and the vault is opened posteriorly using an ultrasonic scalpel by detaching the utero-sacral ligaments. The circumferential incision is completed without a uterine delineator. The vault closure is performed with 1-0 polyglactin using continuous non-locking sutures, ensuring at least two sutures pass through the utero-sacral complex for support, reconstructing the pericervical ring with secure bites to reduce the risk of dehiscence.
